summary: DeVault presents a C implementation of a bloom filter as a response to a C++ blog post, arguing his version is superior in several ways. He designs the filter to accept user-supplied hash functions and arbitrary data types rather than being limited to bytes. The implementation directly manipulates bits rather than relying on C++ standard library abstractions. He walks through each function—creation, hash registration, insertion, and testing—explaining the bit manipulation at each step. The post reflects his preference for C over C++ for low-level data structures, favoring explicit control and simplicity.
key_points: User-supplied hash functions make the bloom filter more flexible and composable than hardcoded hashing
Direct bit manipulation with bitwise operators is preferable to relying on std::vector<bool> for space efficiency
C is a better choice than C++ for implementing low-level data structures, while C++ is better suited for high-level code
Accepting const void* allows arbitrary data types rather than limiting to byte arrays
The entire implementation is compact and self-contained with a clean opaque-type API
money_quotes: I put together a better one in C in a few minutes, and I'll explain the advantages of it.
I chose C because (1) I prefer it over C++ and (2) I just think it's a better choice for implementing low level data types, and C++ is better used in high level code.
Returns false if the item has definitely not been added before. Returns true if the item was probably added before.
This just sets the nth bit of the filter where n is the hash.
That's it! You have a bloom filter with arbitrary data types for insert and user-supplied hash functions.
key_insight: Low-level data structures like bloom filters are best implemented in C with explicit bit manipulation and user-supplied hash functions, giving the caller full control rather than hiding details behind C++ abstractions.
